bug_data.summary: Improve sha256 hashing for downloads' BackgroundFileSaver
bug_data.description:
Based on the conversation in bug 1972941, there are at least 2 things we could do to improve this story:

1. avoid doing this work at all if safebrowsing and DLP are both off (note that this will be a minority case but it seems worth doing even just to make sure we can triage where CPU use is coming from).
2. do not update the hash for every 32KiB chunk of download that comes in, as per bug 1972941 comment 33 that would be better.

Dana, for 2, is there an ideal chunk size for the sha256 calculations, or is just making it, say, 1mib going to be better? Being slightly silly (because I don't understand the overhead tradeoff, so please correct me!) any reason not to just do the hashing once when the file has finished downloading?

comments.author: gijskruitbosch+bugs@gmail.com
comments.creator: gijskruitbosch+bugs@gmail.com

comments.text:
Based on the conversation in bug 1972941, there are at least 2 things we could do to improve this story:

1. avoid doing this work at all if safebrowsing and DLP are both off (note that this will be a minority case but it seems worth doing even just to make sure we can triage where CPU use is coming from).
2. do not update the hash for every 32KiB chunk of download that comes in, as per bug 1972941 comment 33 that would be better.

Dana, for 2, is there an ideal chunk size for the sha256 calculations, or is just making it, say, 1mib going to be better? Being slightly silly (because I don't understand the overhead tradeoff, so please correct me!) any reason not to just do the hashing once when the file has finished downloading?

Doing it all at once at the end would certainly have the least overhead, but if you're downloading a huge file, it would be a bit of a bummer to wait for it to download and then wait some more for it to be hashed. I think it also depends on the download speed - if you're downloading 10s or 100s of MB a second, you probably want to hash in very large chunks, but if it's a lot slower than that, then you don't even have that much to hash. Anyway, I bet at least 1MB or even 2 or 4 (8?) would probably be a big improvement.
comments.raw_text: Doing it all at once at the end would certainly have the least overhead, but if you're downloading a huge file, it would be a bit of a bummer to wait for it to download and then wait some more for it to be hashed. I think it also depends on the download speed - if you're downloading 10s or 100s of MB a second, you probably want to hash in very large chunks, but if it's a lot slower than that, then you don't even have that much to hash. Anyway, I bet at least 1MB or even 2 or 4 (8?) would probably be a big improvement.

comments.text: Moving to Core Networking to evaluate the buffer size change in the appropriate component.

Based on DANA's [comment](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1983225#c1), we can try to increase the [BUFFERED_IO_SIZE ](https://searchfox.org/firefox-main/rev/99d7ebc6bfa5eb28eca5d0d51f80f6788d1b133f/netwerk/base/BackgroundFileSaver.cpp#48) to 1MB and see if this makes any improvement. But we need to ensure we use heap for this

comments.author: smayya@mozilla.com

comments.raw_text:
This patch increases BUFFERED_IO_SIZE from 32KB to 1MB for better I/O throughput
during file operations and hash computation. The read buffer is changed from
stack-allocated to heap-allocated using MakeUnique to avoid stack overflow risks.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

stack-allocated to heap-allocated using MakeUnique to avoid stack overflow risks.

crashes.signature: EMPTY: no frame data available; EmptyMinidump

bug_analysis.root_cause: SHA256 hashing for downloads in BackgroundFileSaver updates hash too frequently (every 32KiB chunk) causing unnecessary CPU overhead, and hashing is done even when safebrowsing and DLP are off.
symptoms: Potential stack overflow risk due to stack-allocated buffer for hashing
reproduction_steps: Download a file with safebrowsing and/or DLP enabled
Note hash updates occurring every 32KiB chunk
bug_analysis.technical_details: The bug involves hashing download data chunks of 32KiB each, which causes overhead. The patch increases the buffered IO size to 1MB and moves the buffer allocation from stack to heap to improve throughput and avoid stack overflow. Consideration was given to skipping hashing entirely if safebrowsing and DLP are off.
bug_analysis.user_impact: Users may experience higher CPU usage during large file downloads, potentially impacting performance and responsiveness.

common_patterns: Crash signature involving family 6 model 183 stepping 1 CPUs
Crashes related to event handling or background processing
Crashes triggered during download or file-saving operations
potential_root_causes: Race condition or use-after-free in event handling code
Improper or unsafe hashing implementation in BackgroundFileSaver
Concurrency issues in DOM event dispatch or background download saving
Hardware-specific behavior or CPU microarchitecture triggering latent bugs
relevant_fix_clues: Bug 1983225 focuses on improving sha256 hashing for BackgroundFileSaver, which may address concurrency or performance issues
No other bugs have detailed status or fix information available
Summary hints at improvements in hashing possibly mitigating crash triggers
similar_bugs_context.summary: These similar bugs suggest that crashes occurring in mozilla::dom::Event::WidgetEventPtr on specific CPU architecture likely stem from concurrency or memory safety issues related to event handling and background file saving processes. The improvements in sha256 hashing implemented in Bug 1983225 may help resolve these by addressing underlying race conditions or unsafe memory operations during download handling.

relevant_fix_clues: Bug 1983225 focuses on improving sha256 hashing for BackgroundFileSaver, which may address concurrency or performance issues

similar_bugs_analysis.summary: These similar bugs suggest that crashes occurring in mozilla::dom::Event::WidgetEventPtr on specific CPU architecture likely stem from concurrency or memory safety issues related to event handling and background file saving processes. The improvements in sha256 hashing implemented in Bug 1983225 may help resolve these by addressing underlying race conditions or unsafe memory operations during download handling.

refined_analysis.root_cause: In netwerk/base/BackgroundFileSaver.cpp, the BackgroundFileSaver::UpdateHash function updates the SHA256 hash every 32KiB chunk using a stack-allocated 32KiB buffer. This causes excessive CPU usage and risks stack overflow. Additionally, hashing is performed regardless of whether safebrowsing or DLP features are enabled, wasting CPU resources when hashing is unnecessary.
symptoms: High CPU usage during large file downloads
Potential stack overflow due to large stack buffer allocation in hashing
reproduction_steps: Download a large file with safebrowsing and DLP features disabled
Observe elevated CPU usage and frequent SHA256 hash computations in BackgroundFileSaver.cpp (UpdateHash function)
refined_analysis.technical_details: Currently, SHA256 hashing updates every 32KiB chunk using a 32KiB stack buffer in BackgroundFileSaver::UpdateHash, causing high CPU load and stack overflow risk. The fix should: 1) increase the buffer size to 1MB and allocate it on the heap, 2) reduce hash update frequency by processing larger chunks, and 3) skip hashing entirely when safebrowsing and DLP are disabled.
refined_analysis.user_impact: Excessive CPU consumption during downloads degrades user experience and may cause crashes due to stack overflow.
refined_analysis.proposed_fix_approach: In netwerk/base/BackgroundFileSaver.cpp, modify BackgroundFileSaver::UpdateHash to allocate a 1MB buffer on the heap instead of the stack to avoid stack overflow. Reduce SHA256 update frequency by hashing larger chunks less often. Add a condition to skip SHA256 hashing entirely when safebrowsing and DLP features are disabled to reduce unnecessary CPU usage.
refined_analysis.optimized_context: In netwerk/base/BackgroundFileSaver.cpp, BackgroundFileSaver::UpdateHash currently performs SHA256 hashing every 32KiB chunk using a stack-allocated 32KiB buffer, causing high CPU usage and risking stack overflow. Hashing runs regardless of safebrowsing and DLP settings, wasting CPU cycles. To fix, allocate a 1MB buffer on the heap, reduce hash update frequency, and skip hashing when safebrowsing and DLP are disabled.
refined_analysis.refinement_critique: The current analysis covers the root cause and impact but is too verbose and mixes symptom context with unrelated details, reducing focus and conciseness. It identifies the key file (BackgroundFileSaver.cpp) but does not explicitly name the relevant function (e.g., UpdateHash) where the SHA256 hashing occurs. Reproduction steps are indirect and mention enabling features that actually disable hashing, causing confusion. The proposed fix is hinted at but lacks a clear, stepwise solution sketch. Overall, the analysis requires a sharper, highly scoped summary with explicit artifact references and a clear, prioritized fix outline to maximize AI-readiness.

filtered_context.concise_summary: In netwerk/base/BackgroundFileSaver.cpp, BackgroundFileSaver::UpdateHash performs SHA256 hashing every 32KiB chunk using a stack-allocated 32KiB buffer, causing high CPU usage and risking stack overflow. Hashing occurs regardless of safebrowsing and DLP feature states, wasting CPU when unnecessary. Fix by allocating a 1MB heap buffer, reducing hash update frequency by processing larger chunks, and skipping hashing entirely when safebrowsing and DLP are disabled.
core_symptoms: High CPU usage during large downloads
Stack overflow risk from large stack buffer allocation
Unnecessary hashing when safebrowsing and DLP are disabled
technical_constraints: Replace 32KiB stack buffer with 1MB heap buffer
Hash less frequently by processing larger data chunks
Bypass hashing when safebrowsing and DLP are disabled
